items.subtopic: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine | APPROACHES TO ETHICAL PROBLEMS
items.title: Balancing Ethical Principles in Diverse Societies
items.content: Physicians must navigate ethical dilemmas by balancing traditional professional codes, ethical principles, and personal values. In diverse societies, different individuals may rely on varied sources of moral guidance, which can sometimes conflict. Physicians should interpret general moral precepts to apply them effectively to specific clinical situations.
keyPoints: Ethical judgments are made daily in clinical practice.
Physicians need lifelong learning about ethical issues.
Traditional codes and principles require interpretation for each situation.
Different sources of guidance may conflict, especially in diverse societies.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine | APPROACHES TO ETHICAL PROBLEMS

items.subtopic: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine | APPROACHES TO ETHICAL PROBLEMS
items.title: Utilizing Ethics Consultation Services
items.content: When facing challenging ethical issues, physicians should articulate their concerns and reasoning, respect others' views, and utilize resources such as ethics consultants or hospital ethics committees. These services can help clarify issues, improve communication, and manage strong emotions, leading to mutually acceptable resolutions.
keyPoints: Articulate concerns and reasoning in ethical dilemmas.
Respect the views of others involved in patient care.
Utilize interdisciplinary team members and ethics consultants.
Ethics consultation services can clarify issues and improve communication.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine | APPROACHES TO ETHICAL PROBLEMS

items.title: Respect for Patient Autonomy in Decision-Making
items.content: Physicians should respect patients' values and informed choices by providing relevant information about diagnoses, prognosis, treatment options, and uncertainties. This involves clear communication to promote autonomy and trust.
keyPoints: Respect entails understanding patient goals, obtaining informed consent, respecting refusals, and protecting confidentiality.
Physicians should avoid withholding serious diagnoses or misrepresenting them due to fear of causing anxiety or depression.
Providing honest information promotes patients' autonomy and trust.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.title: Shared Decision-Making and Informed Consent
items.content: Physicians should engage in shared decision-making by educating patients, answering questions, ensuring understanding, making recommendations, and helping with deliberation. Informed consent involves more than signatures; it requires honest disclosure to promote understanding.
keyPoints: Shared decision-making improves patient engagement and accuracy of risk perception.
Decision aids can help clarify values and goals.
Informed consent is about promoting understanding and choice, not just obtaining signatures.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.title: Caring for Patients Who Lack Decision-Making Capacity
items.content: Physicians must assess decision-making capacity using validated tools and clinical judgment. When patients lack capacity, decisions should be made by authorized surrogates without court involvement unless necessary.
keyPoints: Decision-making capacity involves understanding medical situations and consequences of care alternatives.
Patients with impaired cognition may require surrogate decision-makers.
Physicians determine incapacity for specific health decisions, not courts.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.title: Surrogate Decision-Making for Patients Lacking Capacity
items.content: When patients lack decision-making capacity and have not designated a health care proxy, family members usually serve as surrogate decision-makers. In the absence of available relatives or friends, decisions should follow an institutional or legal process that considers known patient values, goals, and preferences.
keyPoints: Patients may designate a health care proxy through POLST or advance directive.
Family members typically act as surrogates when no proxy is designated.
Patient's current best interests can sometimes override earlier preferences.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 2)

items.title: Maintaining Confidentiality with Exceptions
items.content: Confidentiality is crucial for patient autonomy and privacy, encouraging candid discussions. However, it may be overridden to prevent serious harm when the risk is serious and probable, no less restrictive measures exist, and societal acceptance of adverse effects is minimal.
keyPoints: Confidentiality encourages patients to seek treatment openly.
Exceptions include preventing serious harm or legal requirements (e.g., reporting TB, abuse).
Risk must be serious, probable, and unpreventable by less restrictive means.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 2)

items.title: Addressing Financial Barriers in Patient Care
items.content: Escalating drug prices and patient expenses can compromise care. Physicians should prescribe affordable medications, understand insurance coverage, and follow up on prescription adherence to address cost-related barriers.
keyPoints: High costs may prevent patients from accessing necessary treatments.
Physicians should consider affordability when prescribing.
Follow-up with patients who do not fill prescriptions due to cost is essential.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 2)

items.title: Balancing Evidence-Based Guidelines with Individual Patient Needs
items.content: While evidence-based practice guidelines and performance benchmarking are crucial for improving patient care, they may not always align perfectly with the needs of individual patients. Physicians must be adept at recognizing when an alternative plan could offer substantially greater benefits to a specific patient.
keyPoints: Evidence-based practice guidelines are essential but may require exceptions for individual patient benefit.
Physicians should advocate for reasonable exceptions when necessary.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 3)

items.title: Principle of Justice in Healthcare
items.content: The principle of justice guides physicians to treat patients fairly and make equitable decisions regarding resource allocation. It emphasizes non-discrimination based on personal characteristics and ensures similar treatment for those who are ethically comparable.
keyPoints: Justice means fairness and avoiding arbitrary or biased decisions.
Discrimination in healthcare is forbidden based on race, religion, gender, sexual orientation, disability, age, etc.
Distributive justice requires fair allocation of resources.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 3)

items.subtopic: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine | APPROACHES TO ETHICAL PROBLEMS
items.question: Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a source of guidance for addressing ethical issues in clinical medicine?
options: Ethical principles
Virtue ethics
Professional oaths
Legal statutes
items.explanation: The passage mentions ethical principles, virtue ethics, and professional oaths as sources of guidance but does not mention legal statutes.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine | APPROACHES TO ETHICAL PROBLEMS

items.subtopic: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine | APPROACHES TO ETHICAL PROBLEMS
items.question: What is one way physicians can address challenging ethical issues according to the passage?
options: Ignore conflicting emotions
Utilize ethics consultation services
Avoid interdisciplinary team input
Act based solely on personal values
items.explanation: The passage suggests that physicians can utilize ethics consultation services to address challenging ethical issues.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine | APPROACHES TO ETHICAL PROBLEMS

items.subtopic: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine | APPROACHES TO ETHICAL PROBLEMS
items.question: What is a potential concern associated with newer technologies like artificial intelligence in clinical medicine?
options: Increased patient confidentiality
Strengthened doctor-patient relationship
Concerns about accountability
Reduced need for ethical judgment
items.explanation: The passage raises concerns about accountability as a potential issue with newer technologies like artificial intelligence.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine | APPROACHES TO ETHICAL PROBLEMS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of respecting patients according to ethical principles?
options: Providing information and obtaining informed consent
Respecting patient confidentiality and professional boundaries
Limiting discussions of prognosis or risks to prevent anxiety
Communicating effectively and understanding patients' goals
items.explanation: Limiting discussions of prognosis or risks is not a component of respecting patients; instead, honest information should be provided.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.question: What does informed consent involve beyond obtaining signatures on consent forms?
options: Providing only the necessary medical jargon
Ensuring patients understand and make choices based on honest information
Allowing surrogates to make all decisions for the patient
Withholding serious diagnoses to prevent distress
items.explanation: Informed consent involves disclosure of honest and understandable information to promote understanding and choice.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.question: When can treatment be given without informed consent in an emergency?
options: If the patient is unconscious but has a POLST indicating no emergency care
If delaying treatment while contacting surrogates would jeopardize life or health
Only if the patient explicitly requests immediate treatment
Whenever the physician deems it necessary without considering patient preferences
items.explanation: Treatment can be given without informed consent in an emergency if delaying while contacting surrogates would jeopardize life or health.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is true regarding physicians' obligations to provide care?
options: Physicians must provide any treatment a patient requests
Physicians are obligated to provide interventions that have failed previously
Physicians should not provide treatments contrary to evidence-based practice recommendations
Public policies do not influence medical decision-making
items.explanation: Physicians should not provide treatments without physiologic rationale or contrary to evidence-based practice.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a reason for overriding patient confidentiality?
options: To prevent serious harm to third parties
When an intervention is likely to provide significant benefit
If previous statements do not fit the situation well
When the risk is serious and probable, no less restrictive measures can avert risk
items.explanation: Confidentiality may be overridden to prevent harm or when risks are serious and no other measures suffice; patient benefit alone does not justify overriding confidentiality.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 2)

items.question: In the absence of a designated health care proxy, who usually serves as surrogate decision-makers for patients lacking decision-making capacity?
options: Physicians
Hospital administrators
Family members
Legal representatives
items.explanation: Family members typically serve as surrogate decision-makers when a patient lacks decision-making capacity and has not designated a health care proxy.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 2)

items.question: What should guide surrogate decisions for patients who lack decision-making capacity?
options: The physician's personal judgment
Statutory guidelines only
Patients' values, goals, and previously expressed preferences
Hospital policies
items.explanation: Surrogate decisions should be guided by the patients' known values, goals, and previously expressed preferences.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 2)

items.question: Which principle requires physicians to act for the patient's benefit?
options: Autonomy
Justice
Beneficence
Nonmaleficence
items.explanation: The principle of beneficence obliges physicians to act in the best interests of patients.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following best describes a situation where practice guideline recommendations may not serve an individual patient's interests?
options: When another plan of care would provide substantially greater benefits to the patient.
When the guidelines are outdated and need revision.
When the physician is unfamiliar with the guidelines.
When the patient refuses to follow the recommended treatment.
items.explanation: Practice guideline recommendations may not serve an individual patient's interests when another plan of care would provide substantially greater benefits.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 3)

items.question: What should physicians prioritize in order to act in the best interest of their patients?
options: Strictly adhering to practice guidelines without exception.
Familiarity with relevant practice guidelines and recognizing situations that justify exceptions.
Allocating equal time to all patients regardless of need.
Avoiding any form of advocacy for patient care.
items.explanation: Physicians should prioritize familiarity with relevant practice guidelines, recognize situations justifying exceptions, and advocate for reasonable exceptions.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 3)

items.question: What does the principle of justice in healthcare primarily entail?
options: Providing care based on a physician's personal beliefs.
Ensuring similar treatment for people who are similar in ethically relevant ways to avoid unfair decisions.
Allocating resources based solely on patient demand.
Prioritizing patients with the most severe conditions.
items.explanation: Justice entails similar treatment of people who are similar in ethically relevant ways, avoiding arbitrary and biased decisions.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 3)

items.subtopic: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine | APPROACHES TO ETHICAL PROBLEMS
items.statement: Physicians should prepare for lifelong learning about ethical issues to respond appropriately.
items.explanation: The passage emphasizes that physicians should engage in lifelong learning about ethical issues.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine | APPROACHES TO ETHICAL PROBLEMS

items.subtopic: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine | APPROACHES TO ETHICAL PROBLEMS
items.statement: General moral precepts are always directly applicable to specific clinical situations without interpretation.
items.explanation: The passage states that general moral precepts often need to be interpreted and applied to particular clinical situations.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine | APPROACHES TO ETHICAL PROBLEMS

items.statement: Respect for patients entitles them to insist on any care they want.
items.explanation: Physicians are not obligated to provide interventions that lack physiologic rationale or contradict evidence-based practice.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.statement: In emergencies, treatment can be given without informed consent if delaying would jeopardize the patient's life.
items.explanation: Treatment can be administered without informed consent in emergencies where delay could harm the patient.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.statement: Physicians should prioritize their own self-interest over patients' best interests.
items.explanation: Physicians have a fiduciary duty to act in the best interests of patients, which should prevail over their own self-interest.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 2)

items.statement: Confidentiality may be overridden if there is no risk involved.
items.explanation: Confidentiality can only be overridden when the risk of harm is serious and probable, not in the absence of any risk.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 2)

items.statement: The principle of justice forbids discrimination in healthcare based on personal characteristics like race or gender.
items.explanation: Justice forbids discrimination in health care based on race, religion, gender, sexual orientation, disability, age, or other personal characteristics.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 3)

items.statement: Distributive justice requires that all patients receive the same amount of resources regardless of their needs.
items.explanation: Distributive justice requires fair allocation of resources, not necessarily equal distribution without regard to need.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 3)

items.subtopic: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine | APPROACHES TO ETHICAL PROBLEMS
items.assertion: Physicians may face ethical dilemmas that lead to moral distress.
items.reason: Newer technologies such as artificial intelligence raise concerns about confidentiality and accountability.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine | APPROACHES TO ETHICAL PROBLEMS

items.assertion: Physicians should use validated assessment tools to determine a patient's decision-making capacity.
items.reason: Assessment tools help ascertain whether individuals can make informed decisions for themselves.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.assertion: Patients who refuse treatment should always be assumed to lack capacity.
items.reason: Refusal of treatment indicates a misunderstanding or incapacity in decision-making.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; refusal does not automatically mean lack of capacity, and the reason is misleading.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.assertion: Physicians should advocate for coverage of nonformulary medications when formulary drugs are ineffective or not tolerated.
items.reason: Patients' best interests may be compromised by high drug prices and inadequate insurance coverage.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why physicians should advocate for nonformulary medications.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 2)

items.assertion: Physicians must always follow patients' requests even if they conflict with medical best interests.
items.reason: Respecting patient autonomy is the highest priority in all clinical decisions.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because physicians should balance respecting autonomy with acting in the patient's best interests; the reason is true but does not justify overriding medical judgment.
items.reference: Ethical Issues in Clinical Medicine · ETHICAL PRINCIPLES (part 2)
